stewart_read: Meg captures the bill range at 01:17-01:25 and responds only with 'K.' — the anchor is collected and immediately dropped. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' Meg executes ask and a minimal acknowledge, then moves directly to credit qualification. The bill is never converted into emotional alignment against ComEd. This is the universal failure pattern the schema flags across the corpus.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg may have been deferring the emotional flip to the closer, keeping the setter call lean. Rejected — the flip is a setter-stage move designed to create curiosity before the appointment, not a closer-stage move. Deferring it leaves the customer in research mode through the entire call.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, replay 01:21-01:28 and ask Meg: 'What did you do with that number?' Walk her to a non-arithmetic flip — something like: 'That's real money going to ComEd every single month — that's exactly what we're trying to put back in your pocket.' Flag per schema numeric_discipline: this leverage point would land harder if the rep knew the actual annual figure — coaching gap on financials. The goal is to make the bill feel like a problem worth solving before the appointment is even proposed.

quote: You cannot just send it to me through email or something?
stewart_read: Meg holds the no-pre-meeting-proposal line at 08:00 ('No. We we like to go through it with you. The actual design doesn't have all the information.') — this is schema-compliant. She then immediately offers a phone call as an alternative to in-person, which is a reasonable accommodation, not a capitulation. The schema's hard rule prohibits pre-meeting delivery; Meg does not promise pre-meeting delivery. Two-touch handling: first touch held the line, second touch (09:04) promises post-meeting delivery, which is within policy. Read as schema-compliant improvisation, not a protocol violation.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Meg caved by offering the phone-call alternative too quickly, reducing friction without building urgency. Partially valid — the accommodation was fast — but the hard rule was not broken. The real gap is that neither touch converted the customer's preference for asynchronous review into urgency to show up.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to distinguish between holding the hard rule (which Meg did) and building urgency (which she didn't). The coaching question: after holding the line at 08:00, what could Meg have said to make the appointment feel like the unlock for what Balthazar actually wants? Suggested orientation: 'The design is built around your specific numbers — the specialist walks you through it live so you can ask questions in real time. That's where the actual savings math happens.' No fabricated numbers; the urgency is in the process, not the product.

quote: Yeah. Give me a phone call, Barry, because, I mean k. My my plans keep changing and everything.
stewart_read: The customer names his own fragility — 'my plans keep changing' — before the appointment is confirmed. Meg does not address this signal. She proceeds to confirm the time (08:51) without deploying the 'is that fair' verbal handshake, without anchoring what the customer stands to learn, and without asking whether there is anyone else who should be on the call. The button-up is structurally incomplete: appointment datetime confirmed, phone appointment specified, but no one-hour block stated, no no-driving/at-computer anchor, and no 'is that fair' handshake.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg may have judged that pressing harder on a customer who flagged schedule volatility would increase resistance. Partially valid — but the verbal handshake is low-pressure and predictive of show rate. Skipping it on a customer who already flagged fragility compounds the no-show risk rather than managing it.
coaching_implication: Replay 08:05-08:51 in the 1-on-1. The 'plans keep changing' line is the moment to anchor, not skip. Walk Meg through the full button-up checklist (button_up required elements) and identify which elements she hit vs missed. The 'is that fair' handshake is the single highest-leverage missing piece here — it converts a passive booking into a verbal commitment. Also flag: no co-decision-maker check was made (see side-conversation note at 03:46 below).

quote: Either way, you're gonna send me a copy on everything. Right?
stewart_read: Balthazar's third low-commitment signal — he wants the design in hand regardless of whether the appointment happens. Meg confirms at 09:07: 'Yes. So we'll we'll send you the design afterwards, and you can have it all just for your records.' This is post-meeting delivery, which does not violate rebuttals.send_proposal_before_meeting. However, the promise reduces the customer's urgency to show up: if he gets the design either way, the appointment becomes optional in his mind. The button-up was already incomplete; this promise further softens the commitment without a compensating urgency anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: The promise is a reasonable customer-service gesture that builds goodwill and may improve show rate by reducing anxiety about the call. Possible — but only if paired with an urgency anchor that makes the live call feel irreplaceable. Meg does not provide that anchor.
coaching_implication: This is a handoff brief item, not a hard-rule violation. Coach Meg to pair any post-meeting delivery promise with an urgency anchor: 'We'll absolutely send you everything afterwards — but the live call is where you get to ask questions and see how the numbers work for your specific home. That's the part you can't get from a PDF.' The closer needs to know this promise was made and should be prepared to honor it.

quote: You're going with, AB? Alright. Be careful driving.
stewart_read: Balthazar is speaking to a second person during the bill-collection wait — someone he addresses by initials and tells to 'be careful driving.' This is a co-occupant or co-decision-maker signal. Meg does not acknowledge it, does not ask who is in the home, and does not check whether anyone else is on the title or should be included in the appointment. The verify section item 'title_co_owners' is never completed.
alternative_hypothesis: The side conversation could be with a visitor or a child with no decision-making role. Possible — but the schema's protocol is to ask, not to assume. The cost of asking is zero; the cost of booking a sit where the co-decision-maker is excluded is a structurally vulnerable appointment.
coaching_implication: Replay 03:46 in the 1-on-1. Ask Meg: 'Who was he talking to?' The answer is: we don't know, and that's the problem. Walk her to the verify.title_co_owners item and the protocols.spouse_decision template. The ask is simple: 'Sounds like someone else is there — is anyone else on the title with you?' If yes, the appointment must include them. This is a protocol gap, not a persuasion gap.

quote: I mean, I I wanna schedule it on my calendar then to open up a time slot or not Yeah. When you're gonna call.
stewart_read: Balthazar's act of wanting to block his calendar is a genuine behavioral commitment signal — he is treating the appointment as real enough to protect time for it. This is counter-evidence to a pure 'ghost' read and partially offsets the fragility signaled at 08:05. Meg does not acknowledge or amplify this signal — she simply confirms the time. A brief acknowledgment ('That's exactly right — block it in, we'll be right on time') would have reinforced the commitment without adding pressure.
alternative_hypothesis: The calendar comment is routine scheduling behavior with no special significance. Possible — but in context of a customer who just flagged 'my plans keep changing,' the act of wanting to block time is a meaningful behavioral shift worth anchoring.
coaching_implication: Surface this moment as a positive signal in the 1-on-1 — Meg should recognize when a customer is self-committing and learn to briefly amplify it. The coaching move is not to over-praise but to mirror: 'Perfect — block it in, we'll call right at 1.' This costs nothing and reinforces the verbal handshake the button-up otherwise lacked.

quote: Now the only requirement for our program is that you have a credit score above 670. Is that the case?
stewart_read: Meg states the correct threshold of 670 — schema-compliant, in contrast to the 650 drift observed in SESSION18_fcbf33c1. This is a positive data point worth noting for the manager: Meg has the threshold right. The customer confirms 800 immediately, so no DQ risk here. Positive schema compliance on a threshold that has shown drift on this floor.
coaching_implication: Note for the manager: Meg's credit threshold is correct (670). Use this as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1 before moving to coaching gaps. If SESSION18_fcbf33c1 is also in this review batch, the contrast between Meg's 670 and that rep's 650 is a useful floor-level calibration moment.

quote: K. Yeah. Definitely. You're probably spending a lot in the summertime.
stewart_read: After receiving the bill photo, Meg makes a second attempt to anchor bill pain — 'you're probably spending a lot in the summertime' and 'July and August is pretty high.' This is the closest she comes to an emotional flip, but it lands as observation rather than alignment-against-the-utility. The schema's flip pattern requires converting the bill into a problem the customer feels, not a fact the rep notes. 'You're probably spending a lot' is the rep's inference; 'that's real money going to ComEd every month' is the customer's pain.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg is warming the customer up before the scheduling ask, using the bill observation as a soft bridge. Partially valid — the sequencing is right — but the emotional weight is too light to create urgency. The observation is accurate but not activating.
coaching_implication: This is the second bill-anchor miss in the call (first at 01:25, second here). Use both moments together in the 1-on-1 to show Meg the pattern: she captures the bill data twice and converts it to observation twice, never to urgency. The coaching move: 'What would you say to make Balthazar feel that number, not just hear it?' Suggested orientation (no fabricated numbers): 'That's real money going to ComEd every single month — that's exactly what we're trying to put back in your pocket.' Flag per schema numeric_discipline: this leverage point would land harder if the rep knew the actual annual figure — coaching gap on financials.
